topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white walls of the clinic seemed to amplify the grating sound of my own breathing. I sat rigidly on the edge of the examination table, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white moons against my skin. Dr. Elias Thorne, once the architect of my most painful memories—the one whose sneer had carved permanent grooves into my young psyche—now offered a gentle, measured smile. "Tell me about what brings you in today, Sarah." His voice was smooth, calibrated, utterly devoid of the sharp edge I remembered so vividly. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action a painful effort. "It's nothing," I managed, the words tasting like ash. Internally, a furious churning brewed, a hot, restless energy vibrating just beneath my ribs. I wanted to lash out, to shatter the calm facade he projected, but I couldn't. He was *there*, the one who used to dismantle me piece by piece, now carefully reassembling me with soft words.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the room, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. Elias was leaning forward, his gaze fixed on me with an unnerving, empathetic focus. Every practiced gesture—the slight tilt of his head, the careful modulation of his tone—felt like a deliberate mockery of the cruelty he once embodied. My jaw was tight enough to crack. I wanted to stand up, to scream that the man sitting across from me was a fraud, a polished imitation of the monster I’d once known. Instead, I just stared at the pattern in the rug, focusing intensely on the fibers, trying to siphon off the sheer intensity of my rising agitation. It felt like a pressure building behind my eyes, a need to explode, yet my body remained strangely still, trapped in this agonizing stalemate.

[story 3]

"So, the core of the distress seems to stem from a profound sense of betrayal," Elias murmured, tapping a pen lightly on his notepad. I flinched, a sharp, involuntary jerk of my shoulders. The memory of his mocking laughter—that high, cruel sound—flashed behind my eyes, bringing a sudden, unwelcome heat to my cheeks. I shifted in my seat, the cheap vinyl protesting under my weight. I wanted to stand up and walk out, to throw something, anything, just to disrupt this delicate, controlled atmosphere. My fists involuntarily curled into tight, white-knuckled balls beneath the table. The sheer unfairness of the situation—him, the tormentor, now the supposed healer—sent a violent tremor through my entire frame, a desperate, restless energy demanding release.
